coredata.dc:title: The synthesis of Au@C@Pt core-double shell nanocomposite and its application in enzyme-free hydrogen peroxide sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yayun
Li, Yuhui
Jiang, Yingying
Li, Yancai
Li, Shunxing
coredata.dc:description: A novel Au@C@Pt core-double shell nanocomposite was synthesized and used to fabricate enzyme-free electrochemical sensor for rapid and sensitive detection of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). The well-designed Au@C@Pt core-double shell nanocomposite was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscope (TEM) and energy-dispersed spectrum (EDS). The Au@C@Pt core-double shell nanocomposite modified glassy carbon electrode (Au@C@Pt/GCE) exhibits good electrocatalytic activity towards H2O2 reduction at 0.0V and can be used as H2O2 sensor. The sensor displays two wide linear ranges towards H2O2 detection. The one is 9.0μM–1.86mM with high sensitivity of 144.7μAmM−1 cm−2, and the other is 1.86mM–7.11mM with sensitivity of 80.1μAmM−1 cm−2. When signal to noise (S/N) is 3, the calculated detection limit (LOD) is 0.13μM. Furthermore, the interference from the common interfering species such as glucose, ascorbic acid, dopamine and uric acid can be effectively avoided to H2O2 detection. Additionally, the H2O2 sensor also displays good stability and reproducibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Au@C@Pt core-double shell nanocomposite
Electrocatalytic reduction
Hydrogen peroxide
Enzyme-free sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs, Au@C and Au@C@Pt
Preparation of the Au@C@Pt/GCE, Au@C/GCE and CDs/GCE
Characterization of the CDs, Au@C and Au@C@Pt

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21175115), Natural Science Foundation of Fujian province in China (2016J01067), the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in Minnan Normal University (MX14003), and the Innovation Base Foundation for Graduate Students Education of Fujian Province.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of synthesis process of the Au@C@Pt nanocomposite.
TEM image of CDs (A), UV–vis absorption spectra (Abs) and fluorescence spectra (Ex, Em) of CDs (B).
SEM images of the Au@C (A) and the Au@C@Pt nanocomposite (B), the inset of (A) and (B) are enlargement SEM images of several nanoparticles of the Au@C and the Au@C@Pt.

TEM images of the Au@C (A), one nanparticle of the Au@C (B), the magnification of the selected rectangular area of the Au@C (C), the Au@C@Pt (D), one nanparticle of the Au@C@Pt (E) and the magnification of the selected rectangular area of the Au@C@Pt (F); EDS of the Au@C (G) and Au@C@Pt (H).
XRD patterns of the CDs, the Au@C and the Au@C@Pt nanocomposite.
CVs of the bare GCE (a), Pt/GCE (b), CDs/GCE (c), Au@C/GCE (d) and Au@C@Pt/GCE (e) in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS without (A) and with 1mM H2O2 (B), scan rate: 100mVs−1.
CVs of the Au@C@Pt/GCE in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS containing 0mM (a), 1mM (b), 2mM (c), 4mM (d), 6mM (e), 8mM (f) and 10mM (g) H2O2. Scan rate: 100mVs−1.

(A) CVs of the Au@C@Pt/GCE at different scan rates (from inner to outer: 10, 25, 50, 75, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400 and 450mV/s) in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS containing 1mM H2O2, (B) the relationship between I pc and the square root of the scan rate at 0.0V.

(A) Amperometric response of the Au@C@Pt/GCE with successive additions of 10μL 25mM H2O2 to 5mL 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS at different applied potential (from −0.2V to 0.2V); (B) Effect of the ratio of signal to noise on the applied potential.

(A) Amperometric response of the bare GCE (a), Pt/GCE (b), CDs/GCE (c), Au@C/GCE (d) and Au@C@Pt/GCE (e) to the successive additions of different concentrations of H2O2 (1μM, 10μM, 50μM, 0.1mM, 0.25mM, 0.5mM) in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS at 0.0V; (B) The enlargement of amperometric response of the Au@C@Pt/GCE to several μM H2O2 of curve e in A; (C) plot of response current of the Au@C@Pt/GCE (curve e of A) to the H2O2 concentration; (D) calibration curve of the current response (curve e of A) to the concentration of H2O2 from 1.0μM to 1.86mM; (E) calibration curve of the current response (curve e of A) to the concentration of H2O2 from 1.86mM to 9.11mM, applied potential: 0.0V.
Amperometric response of the Au@C@Pt/GCE at 0.0V with successive additions of 10 uL 25mM H2O2, glucose, dopamine, uric acid, ascorbic acid, H2O2 to 5mL 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS.
Comparison of the present Au@C@Pt/GCE with other enzymatic H2O2 sensor.

head.ce:title: The synthesis of Au@C@Pt core-double shell nanocomposite and its application in enzyme-free hydrogen peroxide sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry & Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry & Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel Au@C@Pt core-double shell nanocomposite was synthesized and used to fabricate enzyme-free electrochemical sensor for rapid and sensitive detection of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). The well-designed Au@C@Pt core-double shell nanocomposite was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscope (TEM) and energy-dispersed spectrum (EDS). The Au@C@Pt core-double shell nanocomposite modified glassy carbon electrode (Au@C@Pt/GCE) exhibits good electrocatalytic activity towards H2O2 reduction at 0.0V and can be used as H2O2 sensor. The sensor displays two wide linear ranges towards H2O2 detection. The one is 9.0μM–1.86mM with high sensitivity of 144.7μAmM−1 cm−2, and the other is 1.86mM–7.11mM with sensitivity of 80.1μAmM−1 cm−2. When signal to noise (S/N) is 3, the calculated detection limit (LOD) is 0.13μM. Furthermore, the interference from the common interfering species such as glucose, ascorbic acid, dopamine and uric acid can be effectively avoided to H2O2 detection. Additionally, the H2O2 sensor also displays good stability and reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) has a wide range of application in the field of clinic, pharmaceutical, food security and environmental protection [1–4]. It is also a vital intermediate in our life process which has been more active and important. Therefore, the sensitive and precise determination of H2O2 is quite necessary and highly appreciable. Many measurement methods, based on spectrophotometry [5], resonance light scattering [6], fluorescent probe [7] and so on, have been reported to detect H2O2. However, many of these methods are complex, time-consuming and expensive. Conversely, the electrochemical method has many practical advantages, including high sensitivity, selectivity, rapid response, low cost, operation simplicity and easy miniaturization etc. So it is essential to pay more attention to the electrochemical determination of H2O2. In recent years, many enzyme-based H2O2 biosensors with good selectivity and sensitivity [8,9] were fabricated, but their applications were limited by the relatively high cost and environmental instability [10].

Recently, various nanomaterials were found catalytic effect towards hydrogen peroxide reduction and used to fabricate nonenzyme H2O2 sensors [11]. The superior sensor performance of the nanomaterials can be attributed to their quantum size effect, excellent conductivity and biocompatibility. Among the nanomaterials, core/shell nanoparticles (NPs) possess various potential advantages, such as good stability, dispersibility, and surface modification, increased functionality, decreased in consumption of the core, and so on. Additionally, the core/shell nanoparticles are different from those of monometallic counterparts [12,13] and alloys [14,15]. Compared with single-metal nanoparticles and alloys, the core/shell nanoparticles not only possess better chemical stability but also have special catalytic properties. Therefore, it is very important to synthesize core/shell nanoparticles and develop H2O2 sensor based on its favorable electrocatalytic activity. The work of Jiang et al. shows strong bimetallic synergistic effect of core-shell Au@Ag NPs, which have much higher catalytic activities than alloy and monometallic NPs [16]. Wang et al. took intermetallic platinum-cobalt core-shell nanoparticles to enhance electrocatalytic activity and stability in oxygen reduction reaction [17]. Zhang et al. synthesized Pt-around-Au nanocomposite based on electrostatic interaction which has been proved as an excellent electrocatalyst for formic acid oxidation [18]. As core/shell nanotechnology becomes increasingly applicable, researchers are beginning to pay more attention to synthesis and application of the core/shell NPs. According to the novel properties of core/shell nanoparticles, they are given rise to immense research interest in various fields such as biomedical [19], pharmaceutical applications [20], catalysis [21], creating photonic crystals [22], etc. The core/shell NPs also have been used to construct H2O2 sensor, including Au@Ag [23], Fe3O4@C-Cu [24], Pt@Au/EDA [25], Ag@C@Ag [26] and so on.

Au, C and Pt are ideal choices of the basal material to construct composition nanoparticles due to their unique electronic and catalytic properties as well as their good stability, convenience of electron transfer and biocompatibility and can be used to develop H2O2 sensor. The core-shell architecture establishes the intimate connection between Pt, C and Au. In this work, we first synthesized Au@C core-shell nanoparticles, and then chose Pt as raw materials to form overclothes around the Au@C nanoparticles. Finally, we obtained the Au@C@Pt core-double shell nanocomposite which looked like popcorns. The nanocomposite was used to fabricate Au@C@Pt modified glassy carbon electrode (Au@C@Pt/GCE). The Au@C@Pt/GCE shows well electrocatalytical activity toward the reduction of H2O2, and can be used for practical purpose in the construction of nanostructured H2O2 sensor. Furthermore, the Au@C@Pt/GCE also exhibits excellent selectivity and stability.

Sodium hydroxide, hydrogen peroxide (30wt.%), polyethylene glycol-200 (PEG-200), sodium dihydrogen phosphate and twelve hydrated sodium hydrogen phosphate were purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Glucose (GL), ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA) and uric acid (UA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Chloroauric acid and chloroplatinic acid hexahydrate were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals are analytical reagents and used without further purification. Redistilled deionized water was used throughout all experiments.

Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by Microwave Synthesis Reactor (Monowave 300, Anton Paar, Austria). The UV–vis absorption spectroscopy was carried through ultraviolet-visible spectrometer (UV-1800PC, Mapada Ltd., Shanghai, China). Photoluminescence (PL) emission was performed using Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorometer (America). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and energy-dispersed spectrum (EDS) were observed with JEM-2100 (JEOL, Ltd., Japan) at 200kV. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were carried out on a Gemini microscope (Hitachi, S-4800, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 10kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) test was completed using Bruker D8 Advance (Germany). Electrochemical experiments were carried out using CHI 650D electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, Shanghai, China). A conventional three-electrode system was used for all electrochemical experiments, which a platinum wire used as auxiliary electrode, an Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) used as reference electrode and a bare GCE (3mm diameter) or modified GCE used as working electrode. All the electrochemical experiments were carried out in N2-saturated 0.1M PBS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs, Au@C and Au@C@Pt
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized according to the reference [27] with little modification. 2.0g glucose solution, 4.0mL water and 10.0mL PEG-200 were mixed to form a pellucid solution. Then the mixture was put into microwave synthesis reactor and kept it at 200°C for four minutes. After it cooled down to 50°C, a brownish red CDs solution was obtained. Lastly, the CDs solution were dialyzed for 3days with the dialysis membranes of 500 cutoffs and diluted to 50.0mL with water, then stored at 4°C for use. The synthesized CDs have carboxyl and hydroxyl groups [28].

Au@C nanoparticles were prepared according to the reference [29] with little modification, and the details are described as follows. 70μL 1wt.% HAuCl4 and 2.0mL CDs aqueous were added to 8.0mL water. The mixed solution was kept at 80°C water bath for 40min to yield a stable red-orange suspension of Au@C nanoparticles. The suspension was centrifuged at 8000rpm for 15min. The precipitates were collected and washed with redistilled deionized water several times and diluted to 2.0mL.

The synthesis of Au@C@Pt nanocomposite which similar to the shape of popcorn as following steps: 0.3mL 0.1M ascorbic acid aqueous solution, 0.3mL 10mM chloroplatinic acid hexahydrate and 1.5mL Au@C suspension were mixed. The mixture was kept under concussion of ultrasonic for 30min until the solution turned to black. Then the suspension was centrifuged at 5000rpm for 10min. The precipitates were collected and washed with redistilled deionized water several times and diluted to 1.0mg/mL. Scheme 1 shows the schematic illustration of synthesis process of the Au@C@Pt nanocomposite

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the Au@C@Pt/GCE, Au@C/GCE and CDs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the GCE was polished with 1.0μm, 0.3μm and 0.05μm α-Al2O3 sequentially to mirror-like and washed successively with distilled water, ethanol and distilled water in ultrasonic bath. The as-prepared Au@C@Pt was dispersed in water with the aid of ultrasonic agitation. Then 10μL Au@C@Pt suspensions were dropped to the GCE surface, and the solvent evaporated at room temperature. The obtained electrode was represented as Au@C@Pt/GCE. For comparison, the Au@C/GCE, CDs/GCE, Pt/GCE was also prepared as the same method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the CDs, Au@C and Au@C@Pt
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the TEM, UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of the CDs. From the TEM image in Fig. 1A, we can find that the CDs particles are amorphous and the particle diameter is about 10nm. Fig. 1B is the typical UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of the CDs. It can be observed a strong UV–vis absorption peak at 277nm. And the fluorescence excitation and emission wavelengths appear at 353 and 453nm, respectively, on behalf the CDs launching yellow-green fluorescence. According to the literature [30], the fluorescence of CDs may be attributed to the raw material of PEG in synthesis process which usually carry electron-donating hydroxyl. These results are consistent with the previous report [31].

The morphology of the prepared Au@C and Au@C@Pt were characterized by SEM. From Fig. 2 A, the diameter of the Au@C particles is about 370nm. After the Au@C core coated with Pt shell, the diameter of the obtained Au@C@Pt nanoparticles is about 420nm and the shape is similar to popcorns, as shown in Fig. 2B. The inset in Fig. 2A and B display the enlargement of several nanoparticles of Au@C and Au@C@Pt, which demonstrate the morphology of the Au@C and Au@C@Pt more clearly.

The details of one particle and the chemical composition of the Au@C and Au@C@Pt were characterized by TEM and EDS. Fig. 3 A–C show the typical TEM images of the Au@C which indicate its diameter about 370nm. It is clearly observed a low-contrast ring associated with a continuous carbon layer wrap around a high-contrast Au core in Fig. 3B, and also proved the formation of core(Au)-shell(C) structure. In order to observe the thickness of carbon layer, the selected rectangular area in Fig. 3B was enlarged, as shown in Fig. 3C. It can be observed that the thickness of carbon layer is about 8nm, which may be formed during the reduction process of Au3+ using CDs as reductant. Fig. 3G provide the EDS of the Au@C nanoparticles, the weight percentage of Au and C were 93.82% and 6.18% respectively, which intuitively demonstrates the particles constitute of Au and C element. In conclusion, the all above results reveal the formation of core–shell structure of Au@C nanoparticles.

Fig. 3D and E show the TEM images of Au@C@Pt nanocomposite. From Fig. 3D and E, the Pt shell is not coated as one particle by one particle, but coated as one nanocluster by one nanocluster, and the shape of the nanoparticle is similar to popcorn with diameter about 420nm. Fig. 3F shows the magnification of the selected rectangular area of Fig. 3E. The thickness of Pt-shell layer outside the Au@C core is about 50nm. Fig. 3H provides the EDS of Au@C@Pt nanocomposite, we can find there contains Au, C and Pt element. From Fig. 3H, the weight percentage of Au, C and Pt was 65.19%, 2.08% and 32.73%, respectively. According to the above SEM, TEM and EDS results, we can conclude that the formation of core-double shell Au@C@Pt nanocomposite.

Fig. 4 displays XRD patterns of the CDs, Au@C and Au@C@Pt. There is a broad peak at around 20° of CDs XRD pattern, which represents the formation of CDs and also demonstrates the prepared CDs is a non-crystalline and amorphous material [32,33]. It is in accordance with the above TEM description of CDs. XRD pattern of Au@C nanocomposite appears four diffraction peaks at 38.34°, 44.52°, 64.88°, 77.8°, which respectively represent (111), (200), (220) and (311) lattice planes of Au [34]. The XRD pattern of the synthesized Au@C@Pt nanocomposite presents seven diffraction peaks of Au (111) at 38.32°, Pt(111) at 40.24°, Au(200) at 44.58°, Pt(200) at 46.88°, Au(220) at 64.88°, Pt(220) at 67.84° and Au(311) at 77.54°, respectively. It is clearly observed the characteristic peaks of Au and Pt. Furthermore, from the peak intensity of the characteristic peak, we can conclude the content of Pt is nearly 0.5 times to the content of Au, which is consistent with the above EDS result.

Fig. 5 A shows CVs of the bare GCE, Pt/GCE, CDs/GCE, Au@C/GCE and Au@C@Pt/GCE in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS without H2O2. There is no obvious redox peak on various electrodes within the applied potential from +0.6V to −0.3V and only present different background current. Fig. 5B shows the CVs of the above various electrodes in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS containing 1mM H2O2. It can be seen the CVs of the bare GCE, CDs/GCE and Au@C/GCE (Fig. 5B, curves a, c, d) display no redox peak and only present small background current, and the Pt/GCE (curve b of Fig. 5B) displays a small reductive peak at around −0.034V, while the Au@C@Pt/GCE (curve e of Fig. 5B) exhibits a sensitive and big reductive peak at around 0.06V. The reductive peak should be ascribed to the electrocatalytic reduction of H2O2. That is to say, the Pt/GCE and Au@C@Pt/GCE display electrocatalytic activity towards H2O2 reduction. The Pt/GCE present weak electrocatalytic effect which caused by the Pt nanoparticles, its mechanism can be represented as [35]:
(1) 2Pt+H2O2 →2Pt−OH
(2) 2Pt−OH+H+ +e→2Pt+2H2O

Furthermore, the reduction peak current of Au@C@Pt/GCE at around 0.06V is much larger than Pt/GCE at around −0.034V. This phenomenon should be attributed to synergy properties of the three elements (C, Pt, Au), the roughened surface formed by the Au@C@Pt core-double shell structure [36] and nanometer size effect [37]. The electrocatalytic mechanism of Au@C@Pt for the reduction of H2O2 can be expressed as follows [26,38,39]:
(3) 2Au@C@Pt(0) +H2O2 →2[Au@C@Pt (1) −OH]
(4) 2[Au@C@ Pt (1) −OH]+H+ +e→2Au@C@Pt (0) +2H2O
In conclusion, the Au@C@Pt possesses well electrocatalytic activity towards H2O2 reduction.

The electrocatalytic activities towards H2O2 reduction were examined by changing the H2O2 concentration. Fig. 6 displays CVs of the Au@C@Pt/GCE in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS containing different concentrations of H2O2. The reduction peak current which appeared at about 0.0V increased clearly with the increasing of H2O2 concentration. The reduction peak potential shifted obviously from 0.06V to 0.0V and the reduction peak becomes broader. It is a typical electrocatalytic reduction process of H2O2 according to the literature [39].

The effect of different scan rates on the electrocatalytic reduction of H2O2 was also investigated. Fig. 7 A displays the CVs of the Au@C@Pt/GCE in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS containing 1mM H2O2 at different scan rates. The reductive peak currents increased gradually with increasing the scan rates. Meanwhile, the reductive peak potential shifted negatively. As shown in Fig. 7B, the reduction peak currents of H2O2 present linear relationship with the square root of the scan rates. The regression equation is I(μA)=2.5 (±0.4) v1
/
2 (mV/s)1/2 +8.04 (±0.03) (n =12, r =0.999). The linear variation of the peak current on the square root of scan rate indicates that the process, which follows a multistep catalytic mechanism in agreement with Eqs. (3) and (4), can be approximated by a diffusion-controlled process.

Fig. 8 A exhibits the current-time curves of the Au@C@Pt/GCE in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS with successive additions of 10μL 25mM H2O2 at applied potential from −0.2 to 0.2V. In Fig. 8A, it is observed clearly that the response currents of H2O2 increased gradually with decreasing the potential from 0.2 to −0.2V, and the burrs of the curves was increased at the same time. Fig. 8B shows the ratio of response current to background current and the applied potential, namely the ratio of signal to noise (S/N) and the applied potential, which increased firstly and then decreased, and the maximum ratio occurred at 0.0V. The current of H2O2 at −0.1V and −0.2V is quite higher than that at 0.0V; however, the corresponding signal of noise is smaller. Thus, an applied potential of 0.0V was chosen as the working potential for further amperometric investigation.

Fig. 9 A shows the typical amperometric current-time curves of the bare GCE, Pt/GCE, CDs/GCE, Au@C/GCE and Au@C@Pt/GCE to the successive additions of different concentrations of H2O2 to 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS. With the addition of H2O2, only small background current was observed at the bare GCE, CDs/GCE and Au@C/GCE (curves a, c, d of Fig. 9A) and small amperometric response at Pt/GCE (curve b of Fig. 9A). However, the largest, well-defined amperometric response can be observed at the Au@C@Pt/GCE (curve e of Fig. 9A) with successive additions of 1μM to 0.5mM H2O2. From the magnified current-time curve of Au@C@Pt/GCE in Fig. 9B, it can be clearly observed that it could achieve the steady-state current within 10s. This indicates that the Au@C@Pt/GCE has an extraordinarily rapid and sensitive amperometric response to H2O2.

Fig. 9C presents the response currents of the Au@C@Pt/GCE to the H2O2 concentration from several μM to several mM. It demonstrates two linear ranges for H2O2, which one is from 9.0μM to 1.86mM with sensitivity as high as 144.7μAmM−1 cm−2 (Fig. 9D), the other is from 1.86mM to 7.11mM with sensitivity of 80.1μAmM−1 cm−2 (Fig. 9E). When signal to noise is 3, the detection limit can be calculated as 0.13μM. The calibration curves in Fig. 9D and Fig. 9E are I(μA)=10.22 (±0.07) [H2O2] (mM)+1.08 (±0.05) (r =0.999, n =33) and I(μA)=5.66(±0.08) [H2O2] (mM)+10.61 (±0.36) (r
= 0.999, n =16), respectively.

From the above experimental results, there is a higher sensitivity at lower H2O2 concentration. The high sensitivity may be attributed to that the H2O2 molecules can freely diffuse with being ignored interaction between molecules. On the contrary, at the higher concentrations, it follows the sorption kinetics on the analyte due to significant sorbate–sorbate interaction [40]. Generally speaking, there is a stronger adsorbing interaction between H2O2 molecules which leads to lower sensitivity. The well sensor properties of the Au@C@Pt/GCE to H2O2 should be ascribed to large proportion of surface active sites and the better electrocatalytical activity of these bimetallic core-double shell nanoparticles. A comparison of the performance of this work with different H2O2 amperometric sensors is listed in Table 1 , including applied potential, linear range and detection limit. The reduction potential of H2O2 at the Au@C@Pt/GCE is higher and linear range is broader than most of other nonenzyme H2O2 sensor. Furthermore, the detection limit of H2O2 achieved at the Au@C@Pt/GCE is among the lowest ones in Table 1. In addition, it is worth mentioning that the linear range and detection limit of the Au@C@Pt/GCE are much better than that the HRP-based H2O2 sensor [45,46], as shown in Table 1. Therefore, combination with the advantages of good analytical performances and simple preparation procedure, the proposed Au@C@Pt/GCE is promising for the development of effective H2O2 sensor.

In the determination of actual samples, there are some electroactive substances such as AA and UA which will influence the accurate determination of H2O2. Therefore, the anti-interference effect of the sensor toward GL, DA, UA and AA was also investigated. Fig. 10 displays the amperometric response of the sensor with the additions of 10μL 25mM H2O2, GL, DA, UA, AA and H2O2, orderly. The current response of H2O2 was far higher than the other interferences. The experimental results indicate that these substances have no obvious interference to the H2O2 determination, which imply the Au@C@Pt/GCE has a good selectivity to the determination of H2O2.

The stability of the modified electrode was also discussed. When the Au@C@Pt/GCE was stored at room temperature for 30 days, it kept current response of 92% to 1.0mM H2O2. In the same concentration, we independently determinate H2O2 with five modified electrodes, the relative standard deviation (R.S.D) is 4.3%. It indicates Au@C@Pt/GCE has good stability and reproducibility.

In this work, core-double shell structure Au@C@Pt nanocomposite was synthesized via a facile approach. Their structure and morphology were characterized by SEM, TEM and EDS. The electrochemical and electrocatalytic behaviors of the Au@C@Pt/GCE were investigated by CV and amperometric technique. The nanocomposite modified electrode performs good electrocatalytic activity to H2O2 reduction and can be used as electrochemical sensor towards the detection of H2O2. The sensor exhibits wonderful sensitivity, selectivity, low detection limit, fast response, good stability and anti-interference ability.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21175115), Natural Science Foundation of Fujian province in China (2016J01067), the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in Minnan Normal University (MX14003), and the Innovation Base Foundation for Graduate Students Education of Fujian Province.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Doxorubicin induces apoptosis in normal and tumor cells via distinctly different mechanisms intermediacy of H2O2-and p53-dependent pathways
Near-infrared (NIR) monitoring of H2O2 vapor concentration during vapor hydrogen peroxide (VHP) sterilisation

Detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose by enzyme product precipitation on sensor surface
